sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content:
Clinical handover is the transfer of professional responsibility and accountability for some or all aspects of care for a patient. Poorly conducted handovers are a leading cause of preventable patient harm. At a FIFO mine site, handover occurs in two contexts: transfer of care to a retrieval team at the site (RFDS, RAC Retrieval, or road ambulance), and verbal handover by phone or radio to a receiving hospital before or during transfer.

This procedure applies to all patient transfers from the mine site clinic. The standard framework is ISBAR.

sections.title: ISBAR Handover Framework
sections.content:
Use ISBAR for all clinical handovers — at bedside, by phone, and by radio.

I — Identity
- Your name and role
- Patient name, date of birth, and gender
- Location of patient at the time of handover

S — Situation
- The primary problem: what is happening with this patient right now?
- One to two sentences: the most important clinical issue
Example: "I have a 45-year-old male with a confirmed anterior STEMI, onset 90 minutes ago, currently pain-free on morphine and aspirin."

B — Background
- Relevant medical history, medications, allergies
- Mechanism of injury or onset of illness
- Any relevant social history (e.g., remote location, language barrier, no next of kin on site)

A — Assessment
- Current vital signs: HR, BP, SpO2, RR, GCS, temperature
- Your clinical assessment and working diagnosis
- Response to treatment so far
- Level of acuity: stable, deteriorating, critical

R — Recommendation
- What does the patient need next?
- What are you requesting from the receiving team?
- Any time-critical actions required: cath lab activation, ICU bed, surgical consult
- Outstanding results pending (ECG, bloods, imaging)
- Your contact number for further questions

sections.title: Handover to Retrieval Team on Site
sections.content:
When the retrieval team arrives on site:

1. Give a structured verbal ISBAR handover before physical handover begins — do not transfer care silently.
2. Continue monitoring and treatment until explicitly told to hand over by the retrieval team lead.
3. Transfer all documentation: patient record, drug register entry for any S8 drugs administered, any printed ECGs or test results.
4. State the drug name, dose, route, and time of any medications administered — verbally and in writing.
5. If the patient has received S8 medications: the quantity administered must be documented in the register before you hand over. Wastage must be witnessed and documented.
6. Answer all questions from the retrieval team — they are not doubting you, they are completing their picture.
7. Confirm the patient's destination and the receiving team's contact details for follow-up.
8. Document the handover time and the name of the retrieval team lead in the patient's clinical record.

sections.title: Phone or Radio Handover to Receiving Hospital
sections.content:
For phone or radio handover to a hospital prior to patient arrival:

1. Identify yourself and the hospital department you need: "This is [name], paramedic/nurse at [site name], I need to speak with the emergency department registrar or charge nurse."
2. Ask: "Are you ready to receive a handover?" — give them a moment to get to a quiet place with a pen.
3. Deliver ISBAR as above.
4. State your estimated time of arrival (ETA) if known, or confirm directly with the retrieval team.
5. Ask for a read-back of the patient name, DOB, and primary problem to confirm they have the correct information.
6. Leave your contact number.
7. Document the handover: time, hospital department, name of person who received the handover, key information provided.

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
The patient record must be complete before handover. It should include:
- Full assessment: history, examination findings, vital signs with timestamps
- All investigations performed and results
- All treatments and medications with times and doses
- Clinician name, AHPRA registration number, and signature

A copy of the patient record (or a detailed referral letter if resources allow) should travel with the patient. Retain a copy on file at the clinic.

Document in the clinical record:
- Time handover occurred
- Who received the handover (name and role)
- Retrieval team or receiving hospital name
- Destination
